title: Post-Event Ticket Chargeback After Date Validation Pattern
narrative: Cardholder purchases premium event tickets ($450-$1200) from secondary marketplaces or primary vendors 45-90 days before the event, successfully validates entry at venue using mobile QR codes or hard tickets, then disputes the charge 15-35 days post-event claiming non-receipt or unauthorized transaction. Typical pattern shows initial purchase from residential IP matching billing addr1, email confirmation retrieved 3-8 times in week before event (suggesting legitimate access), and chargeback filed precisely after the event organizer's 14-day dispute window closes. The ProductCD is 'W' (digital goods/tickets), P_emaildomain frequently personal (gmail.com, yahoo.com), and transaction occurs during business hours (txn_hour 9-17). Card shows clean history with card1_txn_count_24h of 1-2, making fraud detection difficult. Dispute reason code typically RC 13.1 (merchandise not received) despite proof of ticket scan at gate.
indicators: TransactionAmt between $450-$1200 for ProductCD 'W' with MCC 5816 (digital goods/entertainment)
Chargeback filed 15-35 days after event date, beyond organizer dispute window
Email confirmation accessed 3-8 times in 7-day period before event date
Purchase made 45-90 days pre-event from residential IP matching addr1
card1_txn_count_24h shows 1-2 transactions, card1_amt_zscore below 1.5 (normal spend)
Dispute reason code 13.1 (non-receipt) despite venue ticket scan timestamp exists
P_emaildomain is personal free provider, transaction during txn_hour 9-17
summary: Customer buys premium event tickets 45-90 days ahead, accesses confirmation emails multiple times before the event, scans ticket at venue, then files a chargeback 15-35 days after the event claiming non-receipt or unauthorized charge.
reasoning: The cardholder attended the event using valid tickets they purchased, then exploited the post-event dispute window to get a refund while keeping the experience, effectively receiving free admission through deliberate false claims.
indicator_explanations: Ticket purchase sits in premium price tier for digital entertainment events
Chargeback arrives strategically after the event organizer can no longer contest
Customer repeatedly checked confirmation suggesting they planned to attend the event
Purchase originated from customer's home network months before the event date
Card shows stable single-transaction behavior with amount matching normal spending patterns
Customer claims non-receipt but venue systems logged their ticket being scanned
Personal email used during business hours indicating legitimate planned purchase behavior
